item.title: Tomodachi Life: Living The Dream’s Demo Shows The Game Has Basically No Filter
item.content:encoded:
Tomodachi Life: Living the Dream got a surprise demo drop this morning, and players are already testing the limits of what the social sim will let you do. Based on the brief slice people can play right now, Living the Dream seems pretty unrestricted, kinda letting you say and do whatever you want with its Mii creation and Mad Libs-style scenes. Just don’t necessarily expect to be able to easily share screenshots and videos of whatever silliness you get up to.

The original Tomadachi Life was notoriously restrictive on things like gender and dating preferences, but Living the Dream is surprisingly thorough in letting players color in the lines of their Miis, with flexible options for gender, pronouns, dating preferences, formal wear styles, and even nicknames for specific characters. It’s kind of crazy just how deep it lets you get. If you’re looking to create elaborate relationships between your Miis for them to act out, you can probably tweak all the little nuances to your liking.

Funnily enough, all that freedom extends to the dialogue you can have your Miis say, and the game doesn’t seem to have much of a filter on language, either. The real OGs will know that the original Tomodachi Life let people create some extremely funny videos that went on to become memes back in the Vine days such as the “I’m sucking my own dick and dying” song, and it looks like Living the Dream has that same potential.

THERE'S JUST NO SWEAR FILTER??? pic.twitter.com/DVNOoNuERe

— Ori (0/2 SLOTS OPEN) (@oraclesarts) March 25, 2026

I am such a Child I can't believe there's no filtering https://t.co/0zFyE0BI4t pic.twitter.com/ZkKaxLVnDC

— mango (3D C0MMS OPEN) (@EvilTwistedDJ) March 25, 2026

Nintendo’s announcement about restricting some of the share features for Living the Dream is pretty vague, but it seems no one’s having trouble posting videos or images like the above, in which two Miis engage in a passionate discussion about cocks. I can already see this game blowing up on TikTok just like the 3DS one did on Vine. Nintendo would be smart to let players do whatever they want to create viral moments if they want more people to hop on board. Thankfully, Living the Dream is coming to the original Switch rather than just Switch 2, so there will be a lot more potential memelords grabbing the game when it launches next month.



https://kotaku.com/tomodachi-life-living-dream-switch-demo-gender-pronouns-2000681821

item.title: Dispatch developer AdHoc says don't confuse your plot for narrative
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 25, 2026

3 Min Read

Here's a controversial take from the studio behind episodic workplace comedy Dispatch: If you're making a narrative video game then your writing needs to be good. 

AdHoc creative directors and co-founders Nick Herman and Dennis Lenard dropped that bombshell during a talk at GDC Festival of Gaming, and insisted that while it might sound like a big old jape, they were deadly serious. 

The duo explained there is often a lack of specificity when it comes to defining 'narrative' in video games. There are plenty of award shows that dish out prizes for 'Best Narrative' or 'Best Story,' but what do those words mean on a nuts-and-bolts level? Easy. It's about writing, not plot. 

"When you walk out of a movie theater, you don't tell your friends you thought the narrative was great. You walk about the writing," said Lenart. 

Herman tosses out a supporting point: "How many of your favorite TV shows would you say the plot is great but the writing is terrible? We expect more from other mediums than we do our own."

Related:Union workers send a message to game industry execs: 'Let go of the power now, or be forced to let go of it later.'

Lenart believes that when someone begins waxing lyrical about narrative in video games, they should be more specific about what they're actually discussing. Perhaps they're referring to a story players experience through environmental exploration, or a non-liner approach that can be unfurled in any order. 

Narrative is a word of multitudes, and for AdHoc it means "great dialogue." 

"You need to write memorable words," said Herman. "Memorable words mean you'll have memorable characters, and memorable characters mean memorable relationships that people will actually care about." 

Lenart said that great narrative titles aren't defined by plot, but rather the moment-to-moment exchanges that imbue characters with depth. "We intentionally told a very simple story, but with complex characters," he added. "By focusing on writing great dialogue for interesting characters, we knew that no matter what the plot ended up being, we'd have something special and entertaining." 

It's also, he explains, difficult to know whether your writing is good until you have shared it with players. That's why AdHoc leveraged an interactive script tool that allowed the team to playtest the script while it was still being reviewed and revised in the writer's room. "If it's not working on the page, it's very likely not going to work on he screen," continued Lenart. 

It's an exercise that preserved resources while enabling rapid iteration. Herman explained that after AdHoc completed a round of script revisions, it would record voiceover and create a storyboard animatic. Then, it completed one final playtest with around 100 blind testers from around the world using a playable animatic to determine whether the story and characters were actually resonating. Only then would AdHoc begin properly animating a scene.

Related:'Text is evil:' How making Peak changed indie studio Aggro Crab

Lenart said it's an approach that allowed the studio to begin full production with "absolute confidence" in its vision, and said that was only possible because AdHoc takes its writing process as seriously as other studios take their design process.

Game Developer and GDC Festival of Gaming are sibling companies under Informa Festivals.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/production/dispatch-developer-adhoc-says-don-t-confuse-your-plot-for-narrative
